data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: China Hits Back With 125% Tariffs
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

China retaliates against Trump tariffs, raises U.S. goods duties to 125%. (00:24) Lucid wins bankruptcy auction to acquire Nikola’s Arizona factory and other assets. (01:19) Google lays off at Android, Pixel, Chrome amid restructuring - report. (02:21)

This is an abridged transcript.

China has announced an increase in tariffs on U.S. goods to 125%, up from the previous rate of 84%, effective April 12, according to the Finance Ministry.

In a statement, China’s Ministry of Finance warned: “If the U.S. continues to substantially infringe upon China’s interests, we will resolutely take countermeasures and fight to the end.”

The announcement follows the U.S. decision to raise tariffs on Chinese goods. The ministry added that China will disregard any additional U.S. tariffs imposed on Chinese products.

"The U.S. imposition of abnormally high tariffs on China seriously violates international and economic trade rules, basic economic laws and common sense and is completely unilateral bullying and coercion," China's Finance Ministry said in a statement.

Electric vehicle maker Lucid Group (NASDAQ:LCID) has won a bid to acquire select facilities and assets in Arizona previously belonging to Nikola (OTC:NKLAQ).

According to the court filing, Lucid committed around $30 million in cash and non-cash considerations in exchange for Nikola’s manufacturing facility in Coolidge, Arizona; its Coolidge and Phoenix HQ leases; and certain machinery, equipment and inventory.

These facilities collectively add more than 884,000 square feet to Lucid's Arizona footprint. In addition, Lucid plans to offer employment to more than 300 former Nikola employees in roles across its Arizona facilities.

The transaction does not include the acquisition of Nikola's business, customer base, or technology related to Nikola's hydrogen fuel cell electric trucks.

The agreement follows Nikola's bankruptcy auction, which concluded on April 10, as part of its filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy relief.

Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) Google laid off hundreds of employees from its Platforms and Devices division. The Information reported, citing a source with direct knowledge of the matter, that this division oversees key products such as Android software, Pixel smartphones, and the Chrome browser.

"Since combining the Platforms and Devices teams last year, we’ve focused on becoming more nimble and operating more effectively, and this included making some job reductions in addition to the voluntary exit program that we offered in January," a Google spokesperson said, according to the report. However, the company also noted that hiring continues across the globe and in the U.S.

Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

The move is part of restructuring that started last year when Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) combined its Chrome and Android teams under the Pixel and Devices department, headed by Rick Osterloh. The combined company employed around 20,000 people at the time of the merger.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

FDA to phase out animal testing requirement, adopt AI methods

U.S. dollar below $100, Euro at 3-year high as investors flee dollar assets amid tariff frenzy

Hegseth slashes $5.1B in defense spending deemed wasteful by DOGE

Catalyst watch:

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Wells Fargo (WFC), Morgan Stanley (MS), Blackrock (BLK), and Bank of New York Mellon (BK). Options trading implies a double-digit swing when Children's Place (PLCE) reports.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is down 0.5% at $59/barrel. Bitcoin is up 3% at $81,000. Gold is up 0.9% at $3,216.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.4% and the DAX is down 1.5%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Novartis (NYSE:NVS) +3% – Shares rose after the company announced its plans to invest $23B to build and expand 10 U.S. facilities over five years, aiming to produce all key medicines domestically.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 9:00 AM Boston Federal Reserve Bank President Susan Collins will be interviewed on"Yahoo! Finance"

  • 10:00 am Consumer Sentiment

  • 10:00 am St. Louis Federal Reserve Bank President Alberto Musalem will speak on the U.S. economy and monetary policy and participate in a moderated conversation before the Arkansas State Bank Department's 29th Annual Day with the Commissioner.

  • 11:00 am New York Federal Reserve Bank President John Williams will speak on the economic outlook and monetary policy before the Puerto Rico Chamber of Commerce.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Earnings Season Begins With The Big Banks
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Buckle up?

Get ready for the coming flood of corporate earnings, with the big banks ready to kick off the festivities. It comes as the trade war is at the top of mind for investors, with many a portfolio seeing volatile moves in recent weeks. Just how much influence this earnings season will have on markets is up for debate, with half of Wall Street Breakfast subscribers saying it won't have much of an impact.

Snapshot: For Q1 itself, after two "beat and raise" quarters in a row for large-cap banks, Morgan Stanley now expects a "meet and keep" quarter. Given the volatility in markets, "many investors wonder what will even matter this EPS season, as any positive prints will likely be seen as backward-looking and stable-ish outlooks may be met with skepticism," wrote UBS analyst Erika Najarian.

Even if there is less of an influence on share prices, the big banks will provide an important snapshot of the economy. Non-interest revenue will detail the state of capital markets activity, while provisions for credit losses can provide a window into financial conditions. Consumer spending will also be in the spotlight, and don't forget comments on the macro situation - like the recent Treasury market turmoil - from some of the biggest CEOs on Wall Street.

The bottom line: JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon said this week to "settle down and take a breath," and the bank's results will be one of the first out of the gate this morning. Earnings from Morgan Stanley (MS) and Wells Fargo (WFC) are also on tap and commentary there can show how they plan to navigate new uncertainty for divisions like lending, investment banking and capital markets. Will they also factor in a more hawkish Fed in 2025, which can impact expectations for closely watched net interest income?

What else is happening...

Stock roller coaster despite cooler CPI; Gold at new highs.

DOGE review: Hegseth slashes $5.1B in defense spending.

Intel (INTC) CEO's China investments raise security concerns.

AI race: OpenAI said to cut down testing time for new models.

Google to deploy AI to speed up power grid interconnections.

EIA slashes outlook for oil price, demand from tariff uncertainty.

Scotts Miracle-Gro (SMG) cuts cannabis ties after unit transfer.

Greenland takeover: Trump team eyes swaying public opinion.

FDA to phase out animal testing requirement, adopt AI methods.

Additional U.S. tariffs on China to affect Hollywood film imports.

Amazon (AMZN) CEO: Sellers will pass tariff costs on to shoppers.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -3%. Hong Kong +1.1%. China +0.5%. India +1.8%.
In Europe, at midday, London +0.7%. Paris -0.2%. Frankfurt -0.9%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow +0.4%. S&P +0.6%. Nasdaq +0.6%. Crude +0.8% to $60.55. Gold +2.2% to $3,247.80. Bitcoin +1.3% to $82,736.
Ten-year Treasury Yield +1 bp to 4.40%.

Today's Economic Calendar

08:30 AM Producer Price Index
09:00 AM Fed's Collins Speech
10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment
10:00 AM Fed's Musalem Speech
11:00 AM Fed’s Williams Speech
01:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

Companies reporting earnings today »

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Stocks Need Gold, Bonds, Dollar To 'Cooperate'
data.description:
By Mike Larson

What’s it going to take to get a real, lasting rally in the stock market? Cooperation!

Think back to Wednesday. After President Trump paused most of his tariffs for 90 days, the Dow Jones Industrial Average launched its biggest point rally ever. The S&P 500 surged the most since 2008. The Nasdaq? It jumped more than 12%, its biggest single-day rise since the Dot-Com Bubble days.

But gold didn’t give back much of its earlier gains. Neither did Treasury yields. Those turned out to be “tells” - signs of stress elsewhere in the market that suggested equities could be vulnerable. And sure enough, stocks gave back a significant chunk (though not all) of their gains Thursday.

You can see the action in today’s MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows how E-Mini S&P 500 futures (ES) have been trading over the last few days in blue - along with gold futures (GC) in red, dollar index futures (DX) in blue-green, and long bond futures (ZB) in purple.

ES, GC, DX, ZB Futures (4-day % Change)

While other asset classes did react to the stock market surge, those reactions weren’t very powerful. Then overnight and into Thursday, bonds resumed selling off along with the dollar, while gold jumped again.

When you have bonds selling off, the dollar selling off, and gold rising, it tells you something. It suggests “Big Money” is abandoning US assets - and repositioning some of the money into one of the longest-term stores of value.

It’s impossible to know if it’s being done as some form of retaliation (China selling US assets as part of the trade fight). It could just be global investors moving their money elsewhere because they don’t like the uncertainty caused by Trump’s erratic policymaking approach.

Whatever the reason, the outcome is the same. Stock market rallies will remain suspect and “squishy” until equities get some cooperation from other asset classes!

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Q2 2025 Outlook: In The Middle Of The 3% Reckoning
data.description:
As we move through the middle of a fiscal reckoning in the U.S., investors face a shifting macro environment shaped by spending cuts, tariffs and recessionary pressures. In my latest quarterly outlook, I discuss where we are in the cycle, what markets may still be underestimating, and where I’m seeing both risk and opportunity.

  • Fiscal reckoning is here: The U.S. is enacting spending cuts and tariffs that may reduce the deficit by 3% of GDP—roughly $1 trillion—leading to recessionary pressure.
  • Gold and bitcoin remain in bull markets: They continue to benefit from de-dollarization, stimulated by defense uncertainty in Europe and tariff unpredictability.
  • Tech valuations have reset: Semiconductors and growth stocks look more attractive after a major repricing since last summer. Nvidia is now trading around 20x forward earnings.
  • India and international equities are gaining momentum: As the U.S. economy slows, global stimulus efforts are accelerating elsewhere, and India remains a top conviction idea.

A Fiscal Turning Point: How We Cut $1T

We’re now in the midst of what I’ve been calling a fiscal reckoning. Following years of stimulus and deficit spending, the U.S. is transitioning from a “two feet on the gas” economy to a more austere fiscal policy. Last year, the deficit stood at 6.4% of GDP. My base case is that this shrinks by 3% of GDP, or about $1 trillion, through a combination of spending cuts, tax increases and tariffs. Some assumptions and estimates that help us get there:

  • 2 million job losses (400,000 federal workers, 1.6 million contractors): $125 billion
  • Waste, fraud, and abuse savings: $100 billion
  • Tariff revenue increases: $250 billion (conservative compared to estimates of $600–800 billion)
  • Policy rollbacks like ending the Inflation Reduction Act ($65B), reversing Medicaid expansion ($200B), cutting 10% of Pentagon spending ($80B), and modest corporate tax hikes ($55B)

The implications are recessionary, with a potential rise in unemployment to 4.5–5% and pressure on corporate earnings. We’ll start seeing this reflected in forward guidance coming out in the Q2 earnings season, and this lower growth will lower inflation. This gives the Fed room to cut rates, and my expectation is for cuts of up to 200 basis points in 2025.

How to Invest:

  • Stay invested, but diversified—this is a process, not a moment.
  • While a slowdown will likely hit in the second half of 2025, buying should probably start in Q2.

Gold and Bitcoin: Long-Term Bull Markets, Short-Term Caution

Both gold and bitcoin continue to perform well in this environment. Gold has moved above $3,000 and has been the best-performing major asset over the past year. Bitcoin is hovering around $80,000, despite a 10-15% pullback YTD.

Gold and Bitcoin Shine as Top Performers

1-Year Return of Various Asset Classes

Gold continues to benefit from de-dollarization. Central bank accumulation, defense uncertainty in Europe and tariff policy volatility are driving demand for an alternative to the U.S. dollar. That said, I wouldn’t add too much to gold at these levels. It’s above its 200-day moving average, so while I remain long-term bullish, I wouldn’t be surprised to see a bit of correction.

Bitcoin also remains a high-conviction holding in my view, despite its increased correlation with the Nasdaq in the post-COVID era. That makes the diversification case trickier, though not invalid. What’s notable is that bitcoin now outperforms the Nasdaq over nearly every time period, despite recent volatility.

How to Invest:

  • Hold gold, but be ready for a pullback.
  • Maintain long-term exposure to bitcoin; adoption continues to grow for store of value and diversification.

Semiconductors Attractively Valued After a Hard Reset

Last summer, we warned that growth stocks—especially in tech—were extremely overvalued, and I called for reduced exposure. That view played out. Since then, growth has underperformed, and the market has begun to normalize.

Nvidia’s forward P/E has dropped to around 20x, and after about a 30% correction, it now looks far more reasonable relative to its earnings trajectory.

NVIDIA Valuations Now Reasonable

How to Invest:

  • Revisit semiconductors, which offer attractive value.
  • Seek exposure to AI Phase 2 infrastructure plays, including utilities and energy assets.

India: A Structural Growth Stories with Stronger Momentum

The global growth baton is being passed. As the U.S. tightens, Europe is adding fiscal stimulus and China is also stimulating. I believe India remains the most compelling, long-term macro growth story. Following a market correction in late 2024, I believe it’s a good time to increase exposure.

India recently outperformed the S&P 500, and while valuations are high, the P/E-to-growth ratio remains attractive. The Indian economy is not dependent on U.S. exports, and in fact, imports more than it exports. Its growth trajectory is powered by a rising middle class, a strong equity culture and it’s serving as a technology and services base for global corporations.

How to Invest:

  • Increase exposure to India. Its macro story remains intact following its Q4 2024 correction.
  • We favor exposure to growth sectors and digital innovation.

Disclosures

Please note that VanEck may offer investment products that invest in the asset class(es) or industries included in this blog.

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index is intended to track the overall performance of companies involved in the gold mining industry. S&P 500 Index is widely regarded as the best single gauge of large-cap U.S. equities. The index is a float-adjusted, market-cap-weighted index of 500 leading U.S. companies from across all market sectors including information technology, telecommunications services, utilities, energy, materials, industrials, real estate, financials, health care, consumer discretionary and consumer staples. FTSE NAREIT All Equity REITs Index is a free-float adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index of U.S. Equity REITs. Constituents of the Index include all tax-qualified REITs with more than 50 percent of total assets in qualifying real estate assets other than mortgages secured by real property. MSCI Emerging Markets Index tracks large and mid cap representation across emerging markets countries. The MSCI Emerging Markets Investable Market Index (IMI) captures large, mid and small cap representation across Emerging Markets countries. The index covers approximately 99% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each country. MSCI AC World ex USA Index covers a large portion of the global equity opportunity set outside of the United States. It includes large and mid-cap stocks from 22 developed market countries and 24 emerging market countries. Bloomberg U.S. TIPS (1-3 Year) Index measures the performance of the U.S. treasury inflation-linked bond market of obligations with maturities of 1-3 years. Bloomberg Barclays U.S. Aggregate Bond TR Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. Bloomberg Global Aggregate ex USD Index measures the performance of global investment grade fixed-rate debt markets that excludes U.S. dollar-denominated securities. Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. Bloomberg Commodity Index is a broadly diversified index that tracks the commodity markets through commodity futures contracts and is made up of exchange-traded futures on physical commodities, which are weighted to account for economic significance and market liquidity.

Investments in small and mid-cap stocks may be more volatile than those of larger ones, and they are also often less liquid than those of larger companies because there is a limited market for small and mid-cap securities.

Emerging Market securities are subject to greater risks than U.S. domestic investments. These additional risks may include exchange rate fluctuations and exchange controls; less publicly available information; more volatile or less liquid securities markets; and the possibility of arbitrary action by foreign governments, or political, economic or social instability.

Global resource investments are subject to risks associated with real estate, precious metals, natural resources and commodities and events related to these industries, foreign investments, illiquidity, credit, interest rate fluctuations, inflation, leverage, and non-diversification.

Gold investments are subject to the risks associated with concentrating its assets in the gold industry, which can be significantly affected by international economic, monetary and political developments. Investments in gold may decline in value due to developments specific to the gold industry. Foreign gold security investments involve risks related to adverse political and economic developments unique to a country or a region, currency fluctuations or controls, and the possibility of arbitrary action by foreign governments, or political, economic or social instability. Gold investments are subject to risks associated with investments in U.S. and non-U.S. issuers, commodities and commodity-linked derivatives, commodities and commodity-linked derivatives tax, gold-mining industry, derivatives, emerging market securities, foreign currency transactions, foreign securities, other investment companies, management, market, non-diversification, operational, regulatory, small- and medium-capitalization companies and subsidiary risks.

Investments in digital assets and Web3 companies are highly speculative and involve a high degree of risk. These risks include, but are not limited to: the technology is new and many of its uses may be untested; intense competition; slow adoption rates and the potential for product obsolescence; volatility and limited liquidity, including but not limited to, inability to liquidate a position; loss or destruction of key(s) to access accounts or the blockchain; reliance on digital wallets; reliance on unregulated markets and exchanges; reliance on the internet; cybersecurity risks; and the lack of regulation and the potential for new laws and regulation that may be difficult to predict. Moreover, the extent to which Web3 companies or digital assets utilize blockchain technology may vary, and it is possible that even widespread adoption of blockchain technology may not result in a material increase in the value of such companies or digital assets.

Digital asset prices are highly volatile, and the value of digital assets, and the companies that invest in them, can rise or fall dramatically and quickly. If their value goes down, there’s no guarantee that it will rise again. As a result, there is a significant risk of loss of your entire principal investment.

Digital assets are not generally backed or supported by any government or central bank and are not covered by FDIC or SIPC insurance. Accounts at digital asset custodians and exchanges are not protected by SPIC and are not FDIC insured. Furthermore, markets and exchanges for digital assets are not regulated with the same controls or customer protections available in traditional equity, option, futures, or foreign exchange investing.

Digital assets include, but are not limited to, cryptocurrencies, tokens, NFTs, assets stored or created using blockchain technology, and other Web3 products.

Web3 Companies include but are not limited to, companies that involve the development, innovation, and/or utilization of blockchain, digital assets, or crypto technologies.

All investing is subject to risk, including the possible loss of the money you invest. As with any investment strategy, there is no guarantee that investment objectives will be met and investors may lose money. Diversification does not ensure a profit or protect against a loss in a declining market. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

© 2024 Van Eck Associates Corporation

Original Post

data.title: Gold And Other Precious Metals Can Be A Great Hedge For Uncertain Times
data.description:
This article was originally published on the CEF/ETF Income Lab on 4/4/25.

Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the broader market, with a specific focus on how precious metals—namely gold and silver—can be a ballast in one's portfolio during periods of uncertainty. This is a key sector that I use to hedge equity risk, and it has definitely come in handy in 2025 thus far! And, for those who are only recently following my work, I should note that this is a long-term hold for me and not just something I see for our current environment.

Case in point, let us look at one of my preferred ways to play the sector. This is the Sprott Physical Gold and Silver Trust (CEF), which owns both gold and silver in one basket (unlike many passive ETFs that hold one or the other). I reiterated my bullish case on this fund over a year ago, and we can see that performance has been very strong since that review came out:

In this review, I will tackle my reasons for wanting to own gold and silver moving forward more broadly but want to focus on this particular option because it strikingly has a sharp discount to NAV right now. Given the current macro-environment, picking this fund up at these levels seems a bit like a no-brainer in my view:

With that highlighted, I am going to use this article to discuss why I think gold and silver could help hedge some of the trade and tariff uncertainty we are facing in the coming weeks and months. And, furthermore, why I continue to believe in this sector as a long-term hedge for portfolio management. Hopefully, this gives readers some assurance that there are ways to hide out from what could be a turbulent 2025.

**I own CEF and may look to add to my position in the next few trading sessions.

Volatility Has Spiked

Let us start with the obvious—investors are worried. The rattling this week is largely tariff-drive, as President Trump announced sweeping tariffs on countries around the globe, and today (4/4) China responded in kind with an increase in tariffs on US imported goods as well.

This response, in a nutshell, has been quite negative. Equities have sold off, consumer sentiment has taken a nose-dive, and volatility has spiked to multi-year highs in the short run:

The premise here is that none of this is “good”. When investors and consumers are worried, that paints a bleak picture for stocks in the immediate term. However, the silver lining is that there are sectors / ideas that perform well in this type of environment. Bonds are one obvious category, and readers will remember I discussed the merits of municipal bonds in a review roughly one week ago.

But bonds aren't the only play. Gold is often seen as a safe-haven asset, and the reason for that is multifold. The key reason here is that gold tends to perform well when large-cap US equities (as measured by the S&P 500) are in draw-down mode. Take recent selloffs as an example. When the S&P 500 has fallen by noticeable levels, gold tends to deliver positive returns:

The takeaway for me is that gold has a tendency to perform well when times get tough. It can be a good place to find some stability and also to “hide out”, so to speak, when equities are getting rattled. I like to add to my gold position when investors are panicking and then rotate back into equities when I feel the sell-off is on its last legs (which probably hasn't happened yet). Given gold's long-term performance during prior market routs, I feel confident in this strategy going forward.

**In addition to CEF, I own gold in isolation through the iShares Gold Trust ETF (IAU). This is my preferred vehicle for individual investments in gold.

Understand That Silver Is More Volatile

So far, I have mentioned two different funds in this article, and I want to emphasize they are not the same. CEF is a riskier fund in my opinion because it is an actively managed CEF, which inherently presents opportunities and drawbacks. IAU, aside from being passive, is a pure gold play, rather than having both gold and silver. I can see the merits behind both strategies, and it really comes down to investor preference. One is not automatically “better” than the other—it depends on what you want to own!

With this in mind, readers should recognize that silver is seen as a riskier asset—and therefore this makes CEF more risky in my view—because it is not as stable as gold. If we look at volatility measures for a variety of commodities, we see that gold tends to be the most stable. Silver, by contrast, is right at the top end of the volatility range:

There are a variety of reasons for this. But two key reasons come to mind immediately. One, silver does not have the long-standing, historical relationship with mediums of exchange that gold does. In terms of its use globally as a store of value, gold wins the day. I'm not saying silver doesn't have some of that same quality—it absolutely does—just not to the same extent. That makes a bit more volatile.

Two, silver is more economically sensitive because it is used more often as an input for production. Whether it is solar panels, electronics, chemicals, or the number of other ways it finds itself involved in industry, silver's demand rises along with economic activity. When economic activity slows or when there is a rising chance of a recession—such as a right now—the future demand for silver could take a hit. That, again, makes this metal more volatile.

To sum that up, I would urge readers to fully understand what they are buying when they dive into the metals' realm. There are a lot of nuances, and when you branch out beyond just gold—such as through a dynamic fund like CEF or anything like it—you are taking on more volatility and risk. Make sure that aligns with your goals and risk tolerance, as with anything else.

Central Banks Remain Big Buyers

In addition to a “tariff play” and for the relative safety gold offers, I like this idea for the long run for a fundamental reason. We all know the expression "Don't Fight The Fed”, but in this case, I would adjust it to "Don't Fight The Central Banks".

These are two good models to live by, and the latter one is the focus here. What I am referring to is that central banks have been aggressive buyers (and holders) of gold over time. This is especially true of the developed world, but notable mentions include India and China over the last few years as well:

This helps to support the idea that investors can buy into this commodity with assurance it is not a trend or fad that is going away any time soon. Gold is one of the most stable and long-standing options out there, and I expect that story to remain consistent in 2025. When I see trade (tariff) flare-ups or other geopolitical uncertainty, this is often one of my go-to investments. That seems to fit the bill quite nicely at the moment.

Bottom-Line

The market is going through a turbulent time period, and I see gold and other metals like silver as a way to smooth out the ride. It is important to remember that while indices like the NASDAQ 100 have corrected sharply, they are still not “cheap”. This is especially true if the “E” in the P/E equation sees a hit from tariffs. While the NASDAQ 100, as an example, is not nearly as expensive as it was a week ago, it is still not in bargain territory by any means:

I bring this up because I don't see the gold investment idea as being played out. Could it be a little late to the game? Perhaps—but I have owned it a long time and enjoyed a nice run-up, so I am not complaining. Further, today's (4/4) drop suggests that investors are getting a nice little dip if they are looking to add in the short term.

But the bottom-line is that I see equity valuations as still elevated enough to warrant a continuing move towards hedges. Gold is one of the most classic hedges out there, and I will be adding to my position in the days ahead. I hope this review gives investors some confidence to stay level-headed in this market that is causing quite a bit of panic across the board.

data.title: TKO Group Holdings: Remain Bullish Despite Share Price Weakness
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to TKO Group Holdings (TKO) in September last year, with my key thesis being that TKO enjoys a strong secular demand tailwind, and I did not foresee any problems with the UFC/ESPN deal. While the recent share price weakness may have spooked some investors, I actually see it as a buying opportunity. The fundamentals remain intact, and I am more bullish about the likelihood of the UFC/ESPN renewal deal.

Live Events and Sponsorships Demand Remains Solid

At the core level, TKO fundamentals remain strong, and the data from TKO’s recent earnings back this up. I wouldn’t go talk about the entire earnings, but there are some things I want to highlight. Specifically, TKO saw very solid demand strength in live events and sponsorships in both UFC and WWE. Management mentioned that WWE set more than 40 individual market records for ticket sales and paid attendance in 4Q24 and that UFC posted ten all-time highest-grossing event records in the quarter. These do not sound like demand is weakening at all.

UFC Rights Renewal Process Formally Underway

The biggest catalyst remains the UFC domestic rights renewal. As of January 15, 2025, TKO entered its exclusive negotiation window with ESPN, which runs through mid-April. That alone is a sign of progress. While management has not provided guidance on deal terms, I believe the setup is leaning towards positive for TKO.

In that context, I think the recent partnership between UFC and Meta deserves more attention. The UFC named Meta its first-ever “Official Fan Technology Partner” and has plans to integrate Meta AI, Quest headsets, and smart glasses into live events and fighter content. This is a game changer, in my opinion, as it should dramatically improve the fan’s experience—which means better engagement. This deal comes at a very important time, in that it gives TKO a lot of negotiating leverage against ESPN. Some might wonder if the Meta deal pulls eyeballs away from ESPN, but I don’t see it that way. Meta isn’t trying to be a broadcaster— the way I see it is they’re enhancing the UFC experience around the core content, not replacing it. Fans might engage with fighters via AI or backstage VR clips, but the main events still run through ESPN. If anything, this makes the UFC more valuable to ESPN.

For reference, historically, comparable sports media rights renewals (credit to Jefferies research for compiling the data) have typically seen a sizable increase in average annual value [AAV]—ranging from mid-1x to as high as mid-5x (excluding the outlier NWSL). With the META deal, UFC will have a more engaged fanbase, which means more viewership and better retention. Hence, I would argue that the UFC rights will be renewed at above-average levels.

Saudi-Backed Boxing Venture

There is also another growth catalyst that I am positive about. TKO is close to finalizing a strategic agreement with Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund and Sela to launch a new boxing league. The best thing is that this has no upfront capital risk to TKO. In fact, TKO will be receiving >$10 million in guaranteed fees and may receive equity participation based on performance milestones​. This is about as clean and asset-light as it gets, which makes the economics all the more attractive. From a strategic standpoint, this is effectively a free launchpad for TKO to further expand its reach, and if everything works out well (the league scales), this could evolve into a significant recurring growth driver with relatively little execution risk on TKO’s side (since this is the bread and butter of TKO).

Valuation

In my previous model, I forecasted TKO to generate $1.64 billion in adjusted EBITDA in FY26, but I now believe that estimate may be too low, especially in light of the deal with META and data from Jefferies. That said, it is still virtually impossible to accurately size the upside from the deal renewal, and for modeling purposes, I am going to anchor my expectations on consensus FY26 adj. EBITDA in this update. Currently, consensus adjusted EBITDA estimates for FY26 are ~$1.94 billion, implying an EBITDA growth of ~60+% over two years. This EBITDA growth is now in line with Liberty Media Corp. (FWONA) EBITDA growth outlook as well, and if so, I believe TKO should trade in line with FWONA at low-20x forward EBITDA. This translates to ~40% upside based on my model.

Investment Risk

The key risks remain the same for TKO. The UFC renewal deal may not play out as I expected: ESPN could give up this deal or negotiate for cheaper rates. ESPN could also delay this negotiation, which would impact TKO's growth acceleration timeline. Also, while the Saudi-backed deal has little to no financial risk to TKO, this may end up falling through, thereby impacting sentiments around near-term growth.

Conclusion

In my view, TKO's EBITDA growth outlook remains very attractive, and I am bullish that the renewal deal with ESPN will go through at a premium. TKO is doing the right things to extract more value from UFC and WWE brands—as seen from the META and Saudi deals. So long as there are no hiccups to the deal negotiations, I think TKO is on track to see robust EBITDA growth in the coming two years.

data.title: SP500: The Trump Put Is Active, And Possibly The Fed Put
data.description:
The 20% selloff - no more?

The S&P500 (SP500) was supposed to crash on Monday - it was expected to be another black Monday. It was all about the punitive tariffs imposed on April 2nd. Then, on Friday China retaliated, which increased the risk of serious escalation. The risk was that the trade war escalation would lead to a global recession.

The S&P500 opened lower on Monday, at the bear market threshold, with the total drawdown below the 20% level. However, shortly after the market opened there was a rumor that Trump would delay the tariffs by 90 days - and the market spiked higher. However, the White House quickly denied the news as false news. In my opinion, that was the test on what would happen if Trump actually delays the tariffs.

My assessment was that the entire selloff was under control of a single man - the President, and he could reverse the selloff at any time. Further, the Monday low was likely the threshold that President Trump would tolerate, because once the bear market is confirmed, the selloff could intensify and cause a recession. .

On Tuesday, the S&P500 opened higher, but the rally faded due to further US-China trade war escalation. Yet, the Monday low point held, which supported my bottom thesis.

The bond market turmoil

Then on Tuesday night, the 10Y Treasury yield (US10Y) spiked over 4.5%. This was the key development.

On Monday, Japan requested an emergency meeting to make the deal - and this is very important. Specifically, Japan warned the US that it would not be able to continue to invest in US assets if the trade deficit with the US is eliminated.

Countries that have a trade deficit with the US use the US dollars earned from trade to invest in US assets, primarily the US Treasury Bonds (the US has a Capital Account surplus with these countries and the trade deficit matches the capital account surplus). Thus, the US trade partners with trade deficit essentially finance the US budget deficit.

Japan and China are the largest holders of US Treasury Bonds. Thus, Japan issued a warning that the reduction in trade deficit also means the reduction in capital account surplus - and that means the demand for US Treasury Bonds would sharply decrease, at the time when the US would need to issue a large amount to Treasuries to finance the deficit.

Thus, bond market investors got spooked - the US Treasury Bonds sold off, and the interest rate started to spike. A situation like this could lead to a serious financial crisis - with the capital outflow from the US, the crash in the US dollar (DXY), and collapse in the US economy as interest rates spike.

So, what happened next? Trump and his team realized that the situation is about to deteriorate and quickly acted - they delayed the reciprocal tariffs for 90 days, doing exactly what they tested on Monday.

As a result, the S&P500 spiked by 10% in a single day. This was the move I expected, and accordingly I issued a buy recommendation before the move happened.

The Trump Put

Thus, essentially, I have a weak hypothesis that Trump does not want to see the selloff exceed the 20% threshold. It is a weak hypothesis because Trump and his team indicated that they would tolerate a short-term selloff in stocks, and that could mean a selloff in excess of 20%.

However, Trump and his team cannot tolerate the turmoil in the bond market, as this could be a precursor to a major financial crisis. Thus, the Trump administration needs to deescalate the trade war, and there are early indications that there are deals in the making already. In fact, I expect an imminent deal announced with Japan - and this is likely to soothe the bond market.

Thus, as the bond market stabilizes, the stock market is likely to stabilize as well. In other words, the Trump Put is active. That means that any further financial instability is likely to lead to positive trade war news.

The Fed Put

The Fed was clear on many occasions that it will be patient before cutting interest rates - given the strength in the labor market, it can wait to see what the impact of tariffs on inflation will be. Thus, the market participants think that the Fed Put is not active, in other words the Fed would not cut interest rates if the stock market continues falling.

However, the Fed is also concerned about financial stability. I expected that if the Trump administration had failed to respond to the bond market turmoil, the Fed would have stepped in with the QE to buy Treasury Bonds. Given that the Trump administration reacted, the Fed did not have to. However, I suspect that the Fed is ready to step-in in case the bond market turmoil worsens, specifically, if the 10Y yields cross over 4.5%.

Implications

This is not the time to short the stock market, as President Trump can trigger a major rally with a single word, for example with the indication that he will engage in trade war negotiations with China.

This is a highly volatile market for now, and the best strategy is to buy the selloffs and possibly take profits with the spikes. Thus, I am playing the S&P500 (SPX) from the long side. This strategy will be appropriate until we get an early indication of an imminent recession - and at this point the hard data continues to be solid, especially for the labor market.

The risk is to the upside. I expect that the US-China trade war will deescalate, and the current 145% tariff on China will be significantly reduced, especially if the TikTok deal is made.

The current situation is still very fragile, and the selloff on Thursday, after the Wednesday's 10% rally, happened as the US Dollar sharply fell, especially against the Swiss Franc (FXF). The Swiss Franc is the ultimate safe haven currency, and inflows in Swiss Franck reveal the level of fear among international investors.

The Fed has a close eye on the US Dollar, and the Treasury Bonds, and the Trump team understands the danger of the situation. As long as the US labor market holds, that's positive for the S&P500 (SPY).

Here is the S&P500 5-day chart, showing the potential Monday bottom, at least for now. Obviously, the long position has to have a stop loss below Monday's low.

data.title: Dislocations Create Opportunities In High Yield
data.description:
By Bill Zox, CFA | John McClain, CFA | Jack Parker, CFA | S. Blake Haxton, JD, CFA | Dylan Herrmann, CFA

The high yield market has been increasingly dislocated in recent months, meaning some bonds were down much more while others held up much better than we believe was warranted. After the huge declines following the Trump administration’s April 2 tariff announcement, these dislocations are even more extreme. At the close on April 8, the ICE BofA US High Yield Index spread was 457 basis points, the yield-to-worst was 8.63%, and the dollar price was close to 92 cents.

Investors and allocators have to be "comfortable being uncomfortable." In periods of heightened volatility, the comfortable move is rarely the right one over time. We believe high yield is the safest risk asset class, and now is the time for allocators to add or make a plan to add high yield. Furthermore, haven assets like Treasuries and other sovereign bonds could potentially struggle in a pronounced stagflation environment in which deficit reduction targets are missed.

High Yield Weathers Well versus Equities

Some allocators are inclined to sell high yield when they fear recession. We encourage those allocators to focus first on their equity exposure. In significant stock market declines, high yield typically draws down less than stocks and recovers faster. After giving up all of their post-election gains in late March, stocks have been in freefall since the Trump administration’s unexpectedly extreme tariff announcement. While high yield bonds also have been under pressure, they have held up much better than stocks on a total return basis. From the peak of the Russell 2000 Index on November 25, 2024, through April 8, 2025, the High Yield Index is down a modest -2.0% while the Russell 2000 is down -27.9% on a total return basis. From the peak of the S&P 500 Index on February 19, 2025, high yield has declined only -3.3% versus an -18.9% drawdown for the S&P 500 (see Exhibits 1 and 2).

The resilience of high yield bonds relates to their seniority in the capital structure, low duration, high income, and contractual maturity date. At just over 3 years, high yield duration is low compared to stocks and core fixed income and also compared to its own historical range of 4 to 4.5 years. The contractual maturity date of a bond leads to a “pull to par,” which means that as long as default is avoided, the holder gets 100 cents on the dollar at maturity or, in some cases, more when the bond is retired prior to maturity. High yield bonds also generate meaningful current income that can be reinvested at higher yields when the market is weak.

As a result, high yield bonds are more tied to a company’s balance sheet. If the value of the business exceeds the face amount of the bonds, the business should be able to refinance the bonds so that the holder receives 100 cents on the dollar or more. Stocks more closely relate to the income and cash flow statements and the value that the market assigns to income and cash flows. This distinction was most apparent in the last decade for the S&P 500, which was actually 11 years (see Exhibit 3). From the beginning of 2000 to the end of 2010, the S&P 500 compounded at less than one-half of 1% per year, while the ICE BofA US High Yield Index compounded at over 7% per year. The S&P 500 did not catch up until the spring of 2021.

High Yield versus Leveraged Loans and Private Credit

As floating rate assets, leveraged loans and private credit have the wind at their back when the Federal Reserve is raising rates. When rate cuts are the focus, particularly due to a weakening economy, pressure will build on floating rate loans to below investment-grade companies. Lower rates mean lower yields on floating rate loans. In contrast, high yield bonds are fixed rate, and spread widening generally is cushioned by the decline in Treasury yields. From the February 19 peak in the S&P 500, the spread on the High Yield Index has widened by close to 200 basis points, yet the total return is down -3.3%, as of April 8, 2025. Some of that spread widening was cushioned by the 50-basis point decline in the 5-year Treasury yield since February 19.

Allocators Still Prefer CEOs and CFOs over Politicians

The demand for low duration credit of any kind, including high yield, has been strong since late 2023. This increased demand was met with muted net new supply as CEOs and CFOs focused more on preparing for potential recession and higher interest rates than aggressive capital allocation like debt-financed acquisitions, dividends, or stock buybacks. In effect, allocators want to bet on CEOs and CFOs rather than politicians. Inflows into high yield were high during 2024, even when spreads were narrow, because these allocations were strategic rather than tactical. We believe that in most cases these allocations were partial, with the plan to add when spreads widen or yields increase. So far, that is what we have seen in 2025 as inflows into high yield have continued at strong levels as spreads have widened.

Major Changes in Market Structure Create Opportunities

Systematic, momentum, and passive investors have been increasingly influential in the high yield market, particularly in recent weeks. In a down market, assume there are two reasonably comparable bonds, but one is down one point and the other is down five points. What explains the four-point price dislocation? The systematic player is looking for weak small-cap stocks of companies that also have high yield bonds. Upon any fundamental hiccup, the systematic investor typically would short the stock and bonds, putting the company into a negative feedback loop. The stock is sold because the bonds are weak, and the bonds are sold because the stock is weak. Meanwhile, the passive player is a seller of both bonds equally to fund redemptions and replicate the benchmark. In this hypothetical example, the momentum player would short the bond that has sold off more because it is going down. Lastly, the active manager sells the bond that is down one point to buy the bond that is down five points. In recent weeks, however, the market has been dominated by the systematic, momentum, and passive players while the active managers have been less willing to step up.

Risk Mitigation and Opportunity Identification

We are more cognizant of our exposure to the high yield bonds of public small-cap companies that have experienced fundamental hiccups. In our view, the prevalence of systematic, momentum, and passive investors in the high yield and equity markets in recent weeks has created dislocations and a negative feedback loop, particularly for companies with weak small-cap stocks that also have high yield bonds. In a situation like the current environment, we focus on risk mitigation and opportunity identification.

We have always preferred the transparency, price discovery, access to capital, and discipline of the public stock market. What we have found is that when a stock is down 25% to 60% in a quarter, the CEO’s family and friends, the Board, employees, customers, and suppliers all know it. Often, the CEO’s highest priority is to restore the confidence of the bond market to break the negative feedback loop of declining stock and bond prices. We believe this dynamic creates opportunities for active high yield investors. In contrast, we believe that CEOs of private companies are more likely to keep their foot on the accelerator because their stock valuation is not widely known. This thesis, which generally has served us well over time, will be tested in the coming quarters, but we are confident that it is justified.

In the middle of 2022, the High Yield Index bottomed at about a 600 basis-points spread and 9% yield. We believe that was a market that was priced for a very high probability of recession. We started to see capital from various sources, including some private equity capital, come into the high yield market. The High Yield Index has compounded at 8.2% since then. In late October of 2023, the High Yield Index was at about a 450 basis-points spread and 9.5% yield, and the market took off from there. The High Yield Index has compounded at 10.5% since then. We believe we are approaching a similar point today. Further, we continue to see opportunities, and we believe there are plenty of safe 7% bonds, lots of strong 8% to 9% bonds, and some really interesting 10% to 12% bonds, which could have a materially higher internal rate of return. With the slowdown in earnings power, we find these yields compelling in an asset class that is tied to the balance sheet more than the income or cash flow statement like equities.

Nobody knows where current events will settle, but the financial markets, elected officials, the courts, public opinion, and plenty of other forces will come into play. This environment feels to us like one that leads to attractive returns for the long-term investor.

Index Definitions

The ICE BofA US High Yield Index tracks the performance of USD-denominated below investment grade corporate debt publicly issued in the major domestic markets.

The Russell 2000® Index, which is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index, measures the performance of the small-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes approximately 2,000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market capitalization and current index membership.

The S&P 500 Index is a broad measure of U.S. domestic large cap stocks. The 500 stocks in this capitalization-weighted index are chosen based on industry representation, liquidity, and stability.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SoFi Technologies: A Compelling Accumulation Opportunity
data.description:
I wrote on SoFi (NASDAQ:SOFI) recently stating why the investment case was so complicated and made the case that it was prudent to take some profits. Since that time, the S&P is down about 15% while SoFi is down over 35%. Admittedly, it was lucky timing to write a Sell rated article prior to significant policy related volatility, but SoFi was clearly expensive. Much of the bear case that I discussed remains: a declining rate environment, slower lending growth, high default rates, and a cannibalization of its competitive advantage. Yet, the valuation has come back to an attractive level and its now much more sensible to expect positive investment returns in both the short and long-term.

Buy the Dip

The recent selloff offers a very compelling opportunity to begin or add to an existing SoFi position. The company trades at roughly 40x forward profit before tax while expecting 22% forward sales growth and a continued inflection in profitability. Business metrics remain exceptional, with 26% YoY net revenue growth, 34% YoY user base growth, and 32% YoY growth in total products underscored by $23.2b in loan originations. Deposit funding was solid with $25b in deposits, while continued traction in both technology platforms and capital market deals provided a very stable base upon which SoFi can grow.

SoFi is putting on a masterclass on how to win and grow in the digital banking era. The company has no brick & mortar footprint, rather uses a mobile native banking app as its top-of-funnel customer acquisition driver. Users can expect a similar suite of banking products that a traditional bank would offer with the sleek interface and ease of use of a modern Fintech app. Over time, new products and services will be used to create deeper customer relationships and much stickier deposits. Acquiring customers with a low-value, zero cost product and then cross-selling them into higher margin products is not a new concept. In fact, all of SoFi's Fintech peers have taken the same strategy. It works though, and SoFi is executing on the strategy well. The company's Financial Services segment revenue has grown from $167m in 2022 to $821m in 2024. Contribution profit for the segment improved by $500m over that time to reach $300m.

Meanwhile, the company is building very durable revenue streams with its Technology segment, which offers modern backend infrastructure to legacy banks. The product-market fit for Technisys and Galileo is very real. The demand profile for these infrastructure solutions has echoes of the oft quoted and unfortunately true statement that "most of American finance runs on Excel 2003". It may be a tongue-in-cheek comment, but it's woefully true. It's prohibitively difficult to update back-end banking infrastructure in a timely, safe, and cost-effective way. SoFi simplifies this process for enterprise customers (competing banks or Fintechs), which is another key point I brought up in my bearish article earlier this year.

SoFi is effectively modernizing its banking segment competitors with the offerings in its technology segment. The risk is clear: legacy banks have better scale, better brand name recognition, and more capital than SoFi. The entire bull case of Fintech is that modern competitors have an edge in efficiency, driving down cost to serve, which should lead to better unit economics than traditional banks over time. By offering modernized back-end infrastructure solutions to these very banks, SoFi is offering up this competitive advantage in exchange for a durable revenue stream. Product parity with legacy incumbents has a material impact on SoFi's competitive advantage, but the discussion really ought to be focused on whether the revenue stream is worth the loss of that edge. I see it as a risk worth noting but don't believe it will materially harm SoFi. Traditional banks will work to modernize their infrastructure one way or the other, and this business segment at least allows SoFi to make money on this.

Meanwhile, relative to its Fintech peers, SoFi has a much smaller user base. The company has roughly 10 million 'members', compared to PayPal (PYPL) with 434 million users, Venmo with 60 million users, and Block's (XYZ) Cash App with 57 million users. Robinhood (HOOD) has about 20 million users and 25 million funded accounts. Granted, the value of the average SoFi user is likely higher than those competitors, since those apps use a free service as their top of funnel acquisition driver.

The market is clearly undecided. SoFi has a very solid margin profile now that it's a profitable company but also carries a premium multiple relative to the incumbent banks that it's modernizing. It has a very elevated short interest, over 10%, illustrating investor uncertainty. Meanwhile, the company regularly posts double beats and raises in earnings prints, but also regularly trades down post-earnings on sandbagged guidance. The stock has been terribly frustrating to invest despite very solid underlying business performance, but that shouldn't shake out long-term investors. Underlying business growth will result in positive stock returns over time.

My opinion is this: SoFi is a clear Fintech leader with a very differentiated product stack. The user base is sticky, growing, and young. SoFi benefits from its user base becoming richer over time, which begins a flywheel with its increasing suite of banking products. This is a winning equation. Meanwhile, SoFi is becoming a powerhouse in consumer lending and continues happily selling loan books to larger banks to shore up capital. A higher for longer rate environment benefits lenders, but a recession would decrease lending demand. The investment case in SoFi remains very complicated, but I see a positive risk/reward at current levels as the premium multiple is more than justified by premium growth relative to incumbent banking peers. The recent poor price performance means little to the company's long-term story.

The biggest risks remain twofold: 1) SoFi's technology segment allows larger, scaled competitors to reach product parity, and 2) SoFi has a very high default rate relative to legacy banks. That said, I feel management manages default risk appropriately and is not lending irresponsibly. Therefore I do not see default rates evolving into a serious issue for the company. As the user base grows older and becomes higher earning on average, this issue should naturally sort itself out.

Investor Takeaway

The future of banking is digital, and SoFi is a clear leader in digital banking services. That structural tailwind will remain through macro headwinds or recessionary conditions, so there's plenty of reason to believe that SoFi can continue reliably compounding its user base. The financial benefits will follow.

I'm upgrading SoFi back to a Buy as the recent dip presents an opportune time to accumulate this stock.

data.title: SCHX: Stock Market Crash Offers A Great Buying Opportunity For Hefty Gains
data.description:
The stock market crash has created attractive buying opportunities for investors who can tolerate short term headwinds to earn lofty returns over the long term. The selloff has cooled valuations from the recent peak while the potential rate cuts are likely to fuel the rebound in the coming months. Schwab U.S. Large-Cap ETF™ (NYSEARCA:SCHX) appears to be a solid investment vehicles to take advantage of the selloff. This is because the ETF offers access to a portfolio of more than 700 stocks through a single investment. Its low expense ratio, solid liquidity, decent yield and a low risk factor makes it a perfect ETF for buy and hold strategy. Therefore, I initiate coverage of SCHX with a buy rating.

Stock Market Crash Never Lasts for Longer

The US stock market plummeted sharply amid a trade war, sending NASDAQ into a bear market and erasing 17% of the S&P 500 value. The downtrend is expected to last for the next few months because the US market is likely to experience a recession in 2025.

Economists are forecasting a higher probability of a recession. For example, Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley declined their 2025 GDP growth outlook to 1.5% and 1.3%, respectively. Both have also increased odds of a recession to the highest levels in the past two years. Morningstar estimate a 40 to 50% chance of a recession. Moreover, Wall Street continues to slash earnings growth expectations for the first quarter and full year. According to the FactSet data, the S&P 500 earnings are likely to grow around 7% in the first quarter, compared to initial expectations for a double-digit increase.

The situation in the capital and credit market is also challenging. Reports suggest uncertainty has significantly declined activity in the IPO and M&A markets. Furthermore, the banks are expected to report lower earnings for the first quarter of 2025 with expectations that slower economic growth could increase credit losses, decline lending activity and reduce business confidence.

Apparently, the market trends appear scary. However, I believe investors with a high risk tolerance should consider buying beaten down stocks and ETFs during the current bear market. Historical data suggest that bear market lasts on average around 9.9 months, while bull trends can continue for years. The average decline in the past bear markets was 35%. In fact, the stock market bounced back and soared to new highs even after the most painful selloff of the Great Depression and Financial Crisis.

I believe the cheap valuations and rate cuts are likely to fuel a recovery in the coming months and quarters. In the scenario of a slower economic growth, the Fed is expected to slash rates at a faster pace than initial expectations. Goldman Sachs expects a 130 basis points rate cut if the economy enters a recession. In 2020, the Fed adopted a zero interest rate policy to counter recession, which helped in increasing investor confidence and quickly recovering the losses.

Furthermore, the US stock market valuations now appear attractive around 18x forward earnings, compared to the recent peak of 22.5x. The tech sector and magnificent 7 are also trading slightly over 21x earnings, compared to their recent levels of 30x forward earnings. Lower valuations are also likely to improve investor sentiment in risky stocks.

SCHX is a Solid Investment Option

There are various ways to capitalize on the selloff while lowering the risk factors. The strategy of creating a portfolio of few beaten down stocks could be one of the common ways of capitalizing on the potential uptrend while lowering the downside risk. Moreover, considering growth or sector-focused ETFs may also enable investors to earn healthy returns in the recovery. Nevertheless, considering investment options, such as Schwab U.S. Large-Cap ETF, could be a great strategy. This is because SCHX offers access to a large number of stocks through a single investment.

Unlike growth-focused ETFs with a higher exposure to tech stocks, SCHX offers a better portfolio structure, with technology representing 30% of the overall portfolio. The communications and consumer cyclical collectively make up 20% of its portfolio. Whereas, the rest of its 50% of portfolio is spread across the remaining eight sectors. A diversified portfolio always helps in lowering the volatility and other risk factors.

The fund's 31% of portfolio concentration in the top 10 holdings limits its exposure to a single or a group of few stocks. SCHX offers exposure to popular names like Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B), Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Tesla (TSLA), Broadcom (AVGO and nearly 740 other stocks. Furthermore, its portfolio offers a solid combination of growth, value and dividend stocks. According to the SCHX data, if one had invested $10,000 in SCHX in 2015, his investment would have grown to $31,500. This represents a massive 215% return on investment.

The recent share price selloff has made its valuations attractive, trading around 18x forward earnings. SCHX share price is currently trading around the 52 weeks low of $19 per share. In addition, after the 3 for 1 stock split in late 2024, the fund’s lower per-share price can enable investors buy shares in a large quantity. Besides the attractiveness of the share price, SCHX offers low expense ratio, healthy liquidity and a decent yield.

SCHX also earned the strong grades on expenses, dividends and liquidity, according to the Seeking Alpha quant grading system. Its A grade on the risk factor also vindicates my opinion about the ETF’s potential to generate better risk-adjusted returns. SCHX received a hold rating based on the quant score of 2.83. The hold rating is mainly blamed on its low momentum score due to the recent share price selloff.

Other investment options with diversified portfolios are iShares Dow Jones U.S. ETF (IYY), iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB) and Vanguard Russell 1000 Index Fund ETF Shares (VONE). iShares Dow Jones U.S. ETF tracks the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Index. Its portfolio is composed of 1000 stocks, while iShares Russell 1000 ETF and Vanguard Russell 1000 Index Fund ETF Shares track the performance of Russell 1000 index. I prefer SCHX over peers due to its low expense ratio, high liquidity and low per-share price.

In Conclusion

Legendary investors like Warren Buffett have made big profits by investing in a crisis situation and holding those investments for a long time. I believe the current selloff offers one of the greatest buying opportunities in the last two years. However, choosing the right investment option is critical in lowering the risk factor while capitalizing on the potential recovery. SCHX appears to be a solid investment option because of its diversified portfolio, low expense ratio and healthy liquidity.
